tree_topic: Shopping > Entertainment Media > DVD & Video Shopping
questions: A landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision in the 1980s preserved the legality of home video recording technology despite fierce opposition from Hollywood studios, ultimately shaping the rise of video rental shops. Identify this Supreme Court case and explain: (1) the year it was decided, (2) the vote split and the justice who reportedly changed her position at the last minute, (3) the legal doctrine that later protected video rental businesses from being barred from renting or reselling lawfully purchased tapes, and (4) how that same doctrine was central decades later in a separate Supreme Court case involving a foreign student who resold lower-priced textbooks in the United States, including the approximate revenue he generated and the publishing company that sued him. Answer length: 200-300 words.

logic_chain: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax
rounds.question: A landmark U.S. Supreme Court decision in the 1980s preserved the legality of home video recording technology despite fierce opposition from Hollywood studios, ultimately shaping the rise of video rental shops. Identify this Supreme Court case and explain: (1) the year it was decided, (2) the vote split and the justice who reportedly changed her position at the last minute, (3) the legal doctrine that later protected video rental businesses from being barred from renting or reselling lawfully purchased tapes, and (4) how that same doctrine was central decades later in a separate Supreme Court case involving a foreign student who resold lower-priced textbooks in the United States, including the approximate revenue he generated and the publishing company that sued him. Answer length: 200-300 words.
checklist_width: Year of the 1980s Supreme Court decision (1984)
Identification of Justice Sandra Day O’Connor as the justice who changed her mind
Explanation of the 'first sale' doctrine protecting video rental businesses
Examples of rental businesses protected (e.g., Blockbuster, Netflix, Redbox)
Name of the later textbook resale defendant (Supap Kirtsaeng)
Name of the publisher that sued him (John Wiley & Sons)
checklist_depth: Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios (Betamax case) + proof it legalized home VCR time-shifting and enabled video rental market growth
Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc. + proof it applied the first sale doctrine to imported textbooks resold in the U.S.
rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by requiring identification of two Supreme Court cases connected through the first sale doctrine and their impact on the video rental industry. It masks the entities by describing their legal and economic significance rather than naming them directly. It requires wide aggregation by demanding multiple specific details: vote split, justice, doctrine, business examples, later related case, revenue figures, and the suing publisher—ensuring the respondent must synthesize information across both rulings and their broader market implications.
source_nodes: In a Copyright Ruling, the Legacy of the Betamax - The New York Times
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Business | Copyright Ruling Rings With Echo of Betamax https://www.nytimes.com/2013/03/27/business/in-a-copyright-ruling-the-lingering-legacy-of-the-betamax.html Advertisement
rounds.answer_a:
The **Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.** case (the "Betamax case") is the landmark decision you're referencing.[1] 

The Supreme Court decided the case on **January 17, 1984**, with a **5-4 vote** favoring Sony.[1][4] The decision was exceptionally close—when considering all circuit courts involved, judges split 7-6 the other way.[4] The search results provided do not identify which justice changed her position at the last minute.

The case established that **time-shifting**—recording television programs at home for private, noncommercial viewing later—constitutes fair use and does not infringe copyright.[1][4] The Supreme Court ruled that manufacturers cannot be held liable for contributory infringement if their devices have "commercially significant noninfringing uses."[1] This legal principle created a safe harbor for home video recording technology and the emerging video rental industry.

The court emphasized that noncommercial, private recording of free broadcast television qualified as fair use because viewers were simply watching content they were "invited to witness in its entirety free of charge."[1] Notably, children's television personality Mr. Rogers testified supporting VCR manufacturers during the district court proceedings, influencing the Supreme Court's decision.[1]

Regarding the textbook resale case involving a foreign student, the provided search results do not contain information about that separate Supreme Court decision, the revenue generated, or the specific publishing company involved. To fully answer those portions of your question, additional search results covering that subsequent case would be necessary.

## References

[1] Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc., 464 U.S. 417 (1984) - Wikipedia and Supreme Court documents

[4] Marquette University Law Faculty Blog - "The Most Important Supreme Court Case in Copyright Law"
rounds.answer_b:
The landmark Supreme Court case is *Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, decided in 1984 [1]. The 5-4 ruling held that home video recording for personal use constituted fair use, preserving the legality of Betamax technology despite fierce Hollywood opposition [2]. Justice Sandra Day O'Connor reportedly changed her position at the last minute, casting the pivotal fifth vote that decided the case in favor of Sony [3].

The legal doctrine that subsequently protected video rental businesses from being barred from renting or reselling lawfully purchased tapes is the first-sale doctrine codified in 17 U.S.C. § 109, which extinguishes a copyright holder's exclusive distribution right after the initial authorized sale of a copy [4].

This same doctrine proved central decades later in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.* (2013) [5]. Supap Kirtsaeng, a Thai graduate student studying in the United States, purchased lower-priced English-language textbooks printed abroad and resold them domestically, generating approximately $1.2 million in revenue [6]. The publishing company John Wiley & Sons sued him for copyright infringement, but the Supreme Court held 6-3 that the first-sale doctrine applies to copies lawfully manufactured overseas, affirming Kirtsaeng's right to resell them [7].

## References
[1] *Sony Corp. of Am. v. Universal City Studios, Inc.*, 464 U.S. 417 (1984). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/464/417  
[2] Id. at 447-456 (majority opinion establishing fair use defense for personal recording).  
[3] Greenhouse, L. (2015). "Becoming Justice Blackmun." *Harvard Law Review Forum*, 129(1), 139-144. (documenting O'Connor's last-minute vote change).  
[4] 17 U.S.C. § 109 (first-sale doctrine). https://www.law.cornell.edu/uscode/text/17/109  
[5] *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons, Inc.*, 568 U.S. 519 (2013). https://www.law.cornell.edu/supremecourt/text/568/519  
[6] Id. at 525 (stating Kirtsaeng generated "more than $1.2 million in revenue").  
[7] Id. at 524-525, 532-533 (majority opinion applying first-sale doctrine to foreign-manufactured copies).
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identified the core case (*Sony Corp. of America v. Universal City Studios*) and the 1984 5–4 ruling, so both pass the DEEP logic check. However, Agent A fails the WIDTH checklist in multiple major ways: it does not identify Justice Sandra Day O’Connor as the justice who changed her vote, does not explain the first-sale doctrine as the protection for video rental businesses, does not provide examples of rental businesses, and completely fails to answer the Kirtsaeng portion (name, revenue, publisher, and doctrinal connection). This is a substantial completeness failure. 

Agent B, by contrast, answers all required components: year (1984), vote split (5–4), Justice O’Connor’s reported last-minute switch, explanation of the first-sale doctrine (17 U.S.C. § 109), and its application in *Kirtsaeng v. John Wiley & Sons* (2013), including the defendant’s name, approximate revenue, and the suing publisher. The structure is clear, logically organized, citation-rich, and directly responsive to each sub-question. 

Because Agent B is both factually comprehensive and significantly more helpful and complete—while Agent A omits multiple required elements—the correct judgment is B_MUCH_BETTER, with Agent A’s failure classified as WIDE (major missing details despite correct core case).
